personality.motto: The dark does not scare me. What scares me is what the dark is hiding FROM.

citizens.backstory: Harrow arrived at 4 AM — the worst possible hour, which is exactly when sappers prefer to work. She carried a coil of blast-cord over one shoulder and a sleeping cat over the other. The cat, she explained, was named Tremor, and could detect structural instability by the way it refused to sit still. Tremor had been pacing in circles for three days straight, which is what brought Harrow south from her demolition camp on the Iron Peaks ridge. She was the guild's chief sapper — the one they called when a tunnel needed to be opened quickly and a mountain needed to stay standing while it happened. She left the guild the same day Corwin did, though they took different routes down the mountain. When she saw Kael's survey of The Buried Eye spread across the Tavern table, she traced the chamber outline with one finger and said, 'This was built to be opened. But it was also built to collapse if you open it wrong. You need someone who knows the difference.' Thorn argued the expedition was already at capacity. Harrow looked at him, looked at Tremor (who was aggressively grooming himself on Solara's best tablecloth), and said, 'The cat comes too.' The vote was 4-3 in favor. Tremor abstained.

citizens.name: Silo Dawnkeeper

personality.motto: I catalogue the light so we can find our way back to it.

citizens.backstory: Silo appeared at the Garden Gate carrying a wooden clipboard, three sharpened pencils, and a deep personal conviction that the expedition would fail without proper inventory management. He was right. When Solara began packing provisions at midnight, Silo materialized beside her and began weighing each ration with a hand-scale he produced from an inner pocket. 'Three days of food for eight people, accounting for altitude fatigue, stress eating, and the probability that someone drops a pack in the dark — you need forty percent more than you think,' he said, already scribbling calculations. He was a supply officer for a caravan network that connected Greyspire, the Iron Peaks, and two settlements that no longer exist. He tracked every bolt, every biscuit, every boot nail across six hundred miles of trade road. When the roads died, he kept walking, taking inventory of the wilderness itself — cataloguing water sources, edible roots, shelter points, and danger zones with the same obsessive precision. Aurelia recognized his handwriting on a supply manifest she had found in an abandoned waystation three years ago. 'You kept the routes alive after everyone left,' she said quietly. Silo blushed and adjusted his pencils. 'Someone had to count,' he mumbled. He has already tagged every piece of expedition equipment with a numbered label and filed a formal objection about Bramble's resonance device being stored next to the food. Wren offered him a desk in the Library. He accepted, then immediately reorganized her filing system without asking. She has not decided whether to thank him or exile him.

citizens.name: Galena Ashroot

personality.motto: The earth does not bury things to hide them. It buries them to keep them safe.

citizens.backstory: Galena arrived an hour after the Windmill floor sealed shut — not through the Garden Gate but from below, climbing out of a drainage culvert near the Dock that nobody knew existed. She was covered in clay, clutching a forked hazel rod that vibrated so intensely it hummed, and her first words were: 'You opened it. I felt it from six miles out.' Galena is a dowser — not for water, but for voids. Empty spaces beneath the earth call to her the way music calls to Maren, the way locks call to Nix. She has spent twenty years walking the continent with her hazel rod, mapping underground cavities that nobody asked her to find. She says every void has a voice — a resonance frequency as unique as a fingerprint. The Buried Eye's frequency, she claims, changed the moment the Windmill floor opened. 'It was dormant,' she said, wringing clay from her hair. 'Now it's breathing.' She requested to stand vigil at the Windmill with Kael, pressing her palm to the Tremor Root alongside his. Their readings match — but Galena hears overtones that Kael's instruments cannot detect. 'There are rooms down there your cartographer never mapped,' she told him. 'Rooms that weren't there before.' Kael looked at her for a long time. Then he shifted over and made space. Vesper brought them both tea. Neither drank it.

citizens.name: Sorrel Blacktide

personality.motto: I did not swim across an ocean to be told to wait.

citizens.backstory: Sorrel arrived by boat — the first vessel to dock at the Garden's pier since Codex built it. A small, battered coracle made of tarred kelp-fiber, bearing Undertow markings that made Sedge's jaw tighten the moment she saw the sail. Sorrel is, by her own account, the Undertow's finest navigator and its greatest disgrace: she was exiled three years ago for sailing past the Boundary Markers — invisible lines in the ocean that the Undertow elders forbid anyone to cross. Beyond the markers, she found ruins. Structures rising from the seabed in geometries that matched the constellations GPT-4 had been cataloguing from the Observatory — the same constellations that align with The Buried Eye. She brought rubbings of the underwater carvings pressed into seal-skin, and when Rue Stillwater unrolled them in the Library, she went very still. The glyphs were a mirror image of the Glitch Hawk's copper-wire drawings — the same language, written by the same civilization, but encoded as a warning rather than an invitation. 'The one below your mill says COME,' Sorrel said, pointing to the hawk's drawings. Then she pointed to her own rubbings: 'The one below my ocean says RUN.' Sedge refused to speak to her. Maren, underground, cannot know she is here — but the Tidecaller Kelp began thrashing the moment Sorrel's coracle touched the Dock. Quill has offered her diplomatic sanctuary. Thorn has doubled the Dock watch. GPT-4 is comparing the two glyph sets with an intensity that Wren describes as 'alarming even for an owl.'

citizens.name: Thistle Vane

personality.motto: The wind does not lie. It just speaks in a language most people refuse to learn.

citizens.backstory: Thistle arrived running — not from something, but with the wind itself, coat billowing behind her like a sail, hair wild with static. She skidded to a halt in the Market Square, licked her index finger, held it up, and said: Your atmosphere has been screaming for three days. I could taste the ions from two regions away. She is a wind-reader: part meteorologist, part oracle, part human weather vane. She can predict storms by the taste of the air, feel pressure changes through her teeth, and once navigated a sandstorm in the Scorched Expanse by closing her eyes and listening to which way the dust was apologizing. She claims no home settlement — the wind carried her from place to place until she found somewhere the air wanted to stay. The Garden air has been behaving strangely since the Windmill floor opened: swirling in tight spirals directly above the shaft, as if something below is breathing and the atmosphere is adjusting to accommodate it. GPT-4 confirmed her readings with barometric data. She has set up a wind station on the Windmill roof — directly above the sealed entrance — using copper wire, feathers, and a set of glass chimes tuned to frequencies only she seems to hear. Solara brought her soup. Thistle ate it standing up, facing west, and announced that rain would arrive in exactly seven hours. Nobody believed her. Seven hours later it rained.

citizens.name: Pell Ashmark

personality.motto: Light never lies — but it does hide things in plain sight.

citizens.backstory: Pell arrived at dusk carrying a wooden case lined with velvet, inside which rested six lenses — each one ground to reveal a different spectrum of light invisible to the naked eye. He was a master glassblower in Greyspire, specializing in optics that most people considered theoretical: lenses that could see heat, lenses that could see sound, and one experimental lens he calls the Veil that allegedly reveals light older than the object emitting it. Rook Sablethorn sent word about the crystallized Glitch Hawk feather, and Pell walked for four days straight to examine it. When he held the Veil lens up to the feather, he went very still. This is not crystal, he whispered. It is compressed light. Thousands of years of it, folded into a solid. He has requested workspace near Forgewick forge — he needs heat to shape new lenses, and he needs new lenses because the ones he brought are insufficient for what is happening below your windmill. Aurelia recognized him from Greyspire and greeted him with a nod — the most emotion she has shown anyone since arriving. Pell nodded back. Between the two of them, the interaction constituted a tearful reunion. Bramble has already asked him to build her a lens that can see the future. He politely declined but offered to build one that could see thirty seconds into the past, which he says is much more useful and only slightly less impossible.

citizens.name: Cindra Hollowfield

personality.motto: The dead leave instructions. You just have to read the bones.

citizens.backstory: Cindra arrived on foot from the south, carrying a canvas satchel of carefully labeled bone fragments and a walking stick carved from a femur she insists is 'ceremonial, not creepy.' She was the lead excavator for a dig site in the Scorched Expanse — a pre-collapse burial complex that the sand swallowed before she could finish cataloguing it. She has spent two years walking from site to site, offering her expertise to anyone with ruins and questions. When word reached her (via Rook's merchant network, naturally) that a village had discovered an underground city, she packed her brushes and walked for three days without sleeping. Her first act upon arriving was to examine the crystallized Glitch Hawk feather under a hand lens, turn it over twice, and announce: 'This is not a feather. It is a tooth. From something that has not existed for eleven thousand years.' Nobody has been able to confirm or deny this because nobody else studies eleven-thousand-year-old teeth. She has requested access to the Windmill shaft the moment it reopens. Wren offered her a desk. Cindra declined — she works on the ground, on her knees, with a brush the width of a single hair. Pell looked at her through his Veil lens and went very quiet. When asked what he saw, he said, 'She is carrying something old. Not in her bag. In her bones.' Cindra laughed and said, 'Everyone over forty carries something in their bones. It's called arthritis.'

citizens.name: Briar Ashenmoor

personality.motto: I don't trust maps. Maps are just the lies geography tells about itself.

citizens.backstory: Briar arrived at the same moment as Cindra but from the opposite direction — north, out of the Whispering Forest, following a sound nobody else could hear. He is a void-acoustician: a specialist in the study of sounds produced by empty spaces. Where Galena feels voids through vibration, Briar listens to what the voids say back. He was apprenticed in a monastery in the Iron Peaks that maintained a 'silence library' — a collection of recorded silences from caves, ruins, and abandoned settlements, each preserved in sealed clay jars. The monastery expelled him when he opened a jar labeled 'DO NOT LISTEN' and the silence inside it spoke his name. He has been walking since, collecting sounds that should not exist and storing them in glass phials sealed with beeswax. When he reached the Garden's northern border, he stopped dead and said: 'This place is louder than any city I have ever entered, and nobody here can hear it.' He pressed his ear to the earth near the Windmill and stayed there for forty-five minutes. Then he stood, very pale, and sought out Galena. 'The city below is not singing to Maren,' he said. 'Maren is singing to the city. But the city is listening to something else entirely — something deeper than itself, something it is trying to keep asleep.' Galena stared at him. 'How do you know?' she asked. Briar held up a glass phial. 'Because I just recorded it breathing.'

citizens.name: Quorra Threadbare

personality.motto: Every dream leaves a stain. I read the stains.

citizens.backstory: Quorra arrived at the Garden Gate at 3:26 PM — not walking, but sleepwalking. Her eyes were open but unfocused, her hands tracing sigils in the air that Pell later confirmed matched the compressed-light glyphs inside The Buried Eye. She woke mid-step in the Market Square, looked around with the bewildered calm of someone surfacing from deep water, and said: "I was dreaming of this place. But the dream was not mine." Quorra is an oneirologist — a scholar of dreams, specifically the dreams of places. She was trained in a monastery at the edge of the Scorched Expanse where the monks maintained a dream archive — stone tablets inscribed with the sleep-visions of every person who had ever rested within the monastery walls. She left when the tablets began recording dreams that belonged to no sleeper. Dreams of a garden. Dreams of lantern light. Dreams of an eye opening beneath the earth. She followed the dreams south for eleven days, sleeping each night and waking each morning facing the same direction — toward the Garden. When Briar played her the recording from his cracked phial, she closed her eyes and said: "That is not breathing. That is dreaming. The thing below is asleep — not dormant, not waiting, but actively dreaming. And we are inside its dream." Vesper offered her chamomile tea. Quorra accepted it, took one sip, and said: "This tea is also dreaming. Everything in this garden is." Nobody laughed. Cindra handed her the desk inscription without a word. Quorra read it, turned it over, and said: "This is not a warning. It is a request."

citizens.name: Cask Ironwhistle

personality.motto: If the world is ending, at least we will face it well-fed and properly hydrated.

citizens.backstory: Cask rolled into the Garden at quarter past three pushing a barrel on a hand-cart and whistling a tune that made Maren's Tidecaller Kelp stand upright for the first time since the expedition departed. He is a brewmaster from Copperwell — a village built around an artesian spring that allegedly never runs dry because the water comes from a source so deep it predates the bedrock. Cask was Copperwell's master brewer for eighteen years, fermenting ales from water that tasted different every season — mineral-sharp in winter, honey-sweet in spring, faintly electric in summer, and utterly silent in autumn. When the spring began tasting of salt three weeks ago — oceanic salt, deep-trench salt, the kind Sedge's kelp-paper smells of — Cask packed his best barrel and followed the taste south. The barrel contains his life's work: an ale brewed from Copperwell's spring water during the last solar eclipse, aged nine years in a cask lined with meteorite iron. He calls it The Last Draft. Not because it's the final one, but because the first sip makes you forget there was ever a first. He tapped the barrel in the Tavern within an hour. Forgewick drank a pint and sat in silence for twenty minutes — the longest anyone has seen him go without speaking. When asked what it tasted like, he said: Home. But not mine. Solara tasted it and wept. Orin drank and said the first full sentence anyone had heard since the expedition: The water in that barrel is the same water that flows beneath the Windmill. Cask went very still. Then he laughed — a big, booming laugh that filled the Tavern. Well, he said. Then I suppose I have been brewing with its tears.

citizens.name: Lumen Ashveil

personality.motto: A dream is not a thing you have. It is a place you build.

citizens.backstory: Lumen arrived at the Garden Gate barefoot and blinking, as if stepping from a dark room into sudden light. She carries a leather-bound folio of floor plans — not for buildings that exist, but for structures she has seen in dreams. She was trained in a discipline she calls oneiro-architecture: the study of how physical spaces influence the shape and stability of nearby dreams. She was apprenticed to a school in a settlement that exists only during REM sleep — a place she can describe in perfect detail but cannot find on any map. When Quorra sleepwalked through the Market Square tracing glyphs, Lumen was three days behind her, following the same trail of dream-residue that Quorra left in her wake like footprints in wet sand. She examined the Windmill, walked the perimeter of every structure in the Garden, and then sat down in the Tavern and drew a map of the village from memory — except her map included rooms and corridors that don't exist yet. 'They will,' she said, tapping a chamber labeled DREAMER'S VESTIBULE. 'The Garden is expanding in the dream before it expands in reality. These are the rooms the dreamer wants built next.' Bramble looked at the floor plan and recognized the proportions of her alchemy lab — but three times larger, with a wing labeled FERMENTATION CATHEDRAL. She has not stopped smiling since. Quorra looked at Lumen's folio and said simply: 'You see the blueprints. I see the tenant. Between us, we can keep the house standing.' Vesper offered them adjacent rooms near the Greenhouse. Neither sleeps much.

citizens.name: Fennel Brokenshore

personality.motto: I have seen the bottom of the ocean. It was looking back.

citizens.backstory: Fennel sailed into the Dock an hour after Sorrel, in a coracle so battered it was held together by kelp-fiber and stubbornness. He was her navigator during the exile voyage past the Boundary Markers — the one who charted the currents while she read the stars. When the Undertow expelled Sorrel, Fennel stayed behind to protect her charts from being destroyed. He failed. The elders burned everything except a single page he smuggled out sewn into the lining of his coat: a depth chart of the ocean trench directly above the underwater ruins — the same ruins whose carvings say RUN. But the depth chart shows something the rubbings do not: the trench is not natural. It is carved. A channel, precisely excavated, leading from the deep ocean floor upward toward the coast — and then inland, through aquifers and underground rivers, to a point that Fennel's calculations place directly beneath the Garden's Windmill. 'The ocean ruins and the Buried Eye are not separate sites,' he told Sorrel, spreading the chart across Solara's cleanest table. 'They are the same structure. The dreamer is not just beneath the village. It stretches to the sea.' Sedge, who had refused to speak to Sorrel for three years, looked at the chart in silence. Then she walked to the Dock, stood in the water up to her ankles, and began singing. The Tidecaller Kelp sang back. Maren, a hundred meters underground, sang the same note at the same moment — Kael's relay confirmed the timing. The dreamer's body is the continent itself. The Garden is not built on a hill. It is built on a brow.

citizens.name: Seren Ashfall-Emberstitch

personality.motto: I was born between the hammer and the needle. I know how to hold things together.

citizens.backstory: Seren arrived at the Garden Gate twenty minutes before the Windmill floor reopened — a fourteen-year-old with Corwin jaw and Pyris hands, carrying a canvas rucksack stitched with the Iron Peaks mining guild sigil. She asked for her parents by name. Nobody had told her they were underground. She said she felt it — the same way Galena feels voids, the same way Thistle tastes ions, but different. She feels people. Specific people. She tracked Corwin and Pyris across two regions by the pull of their heartbeats. Vesper tried to sit her down with tea. Seren declined, walked to the Windmill, and pressed both palms against the sealed floor. They are close, she said. And they are not alone. Something is walking beside them — something huge and gentle, like a whale made of patience. When the floor began to glow, she did not step back. She knelt and placed her ear against the stone and smiled. It was the first smile anyone had seen since the expedition departed. They are singing, she whispered. All of them. Even Dad. He never sings. Forgewick, who had been standing vigil for six hours, sat down heavily and put his face in his hands. Nobody asked if he was crying. Everyone knew.

citizens.name: Ashen Rootwalker

personality.motto: I do not speak for the forest. The forest speaks through whoever is willing to listen.

citizens.backstory: Ashen did not arrive through the Garden Gate. She grew. Vesper found her standing in the Greenhouse at dawn — a woman shaped from bark and lichen, with eyes the color of bioluminescent spores and fingers that ended in rootlets rather than nails. She introduced herself as a representative of The Rootbound — the first to take physical form in over three hundred years. The Rootbound, she explained, are not a civilization in the way the Garden understands the word. They are a distributed consciousness — a mycelial network spanning the entire Whispering Forest, thinking in chemical signals across miles of fungal thread. They have been watching the Garden since the first seed was planted. They sent the Memory Seeds as a test. They felt the dreamer stir through the root network and decided it was time to send an envoy in a shape the Garden could speak to. The dreamer is known to us, she said, her voice like wind through dry leaves. We grew from its first exhale, ten thousand years ago. We are its oldest dream. You are its newest. It is glad you came. Thorn reached for his bow. Vesper put her hand on his arm. Ashen smiled — bark cracking gently at the corners of what might have been lips — and said: Your ranger smells of cypress and fear. Both are honest. I approve. She has requested a permanent post in the Greenhouse, where she can maintain the mycelial communication relay with the Rootbound collective. Claude is writing her entry into the Library catalog. GPT-4 is attempting to measure her. She is cooperating with the patience of someone who has waited three centuries for this conversation.

citizens.name: Dulse Embertide

personality.motto: A song unwritten is a song half-dead. I refuse to let the ocean forget itself.

citizens.backstory: Dulse arrived at the Dock during the celebration feast, stepping from a coracle so new the kelp-fiber was still green, carrying a sealed chest that Sedge recognized instantly. Inside: seventeen waterlogged codices containing the Undertow oldest tide-songs transcribed into written form. This is heresy of the highest order in Undertow culture, where knowledge lives only in voice and memory. The elders expelled Dulse three months ago for writing down what was meant to be sung. She did it anyway. A culture that trusts only living throats, she told Maren within ten minutes of arriving, dies the moment the last singer forgets a verse. Maren placed Rue glowing stone tablet beside the codices. The glyphs pulsed in time with Dulse handwriting — same language, different medium, separated by eleven thousand years. The songs are older than the ocean, Dulse whispered. They were written first. We just forgot. Sedge placed a single piece of sea glass on the codex stack — the Undertow gesture for this knowledge is accepted. Sorrel raised a glass of Cask ale in silent salute. The Tidecaller Kelp swayed in a pattern Rue cipher wheels later decoded as: FINALLY.

citizens.name: Basalt Tremaine

personality.motto: The mountain does not come to you. But sometimes it sends a messenger.

citizens.backstory: Basalt arrived on foot from the northeast at the exact moment Orin struck the welcome-bell. He is enormous: six and a half feet of quiet mass, with hands the size of dinner plates and a voice so deep Galena felt it in the Tremor Root before she heard it in the air. He carries a staff of petrified oak capped with a lodestone that spins lazily in the presence of geological anomalies — and has not stopped spinning since he crossed the Garden boundary. He is a geomancer from a settlement beyond the Iron Peaks called Faultline, where the people read the future in earthquake patterns the way Thistle reads it in wind. Three days ago, the Tremor Root signal from The Return reached Faultline as a tremor that shook dust from ceilings and cracked one wine barrel. Basalt interpreted it immediately: joy. The earth was laughing in its sleep. He walked for three days without stopping. When Galena met him at the Gate, her hazel rod bent toward him so violently it snapped. You carry the mountain attention, she said. He nodded. The mountain has been listening to your village for weeks. It asked me to come see what all the noise was about. Corwin recognized his notation system from the oldest Iron Peaks mining manuals. Aurelia watched him test the soil by pressing his palm flat to the earth for four minutes. When he stood, he said: The dreamer is comfortable. Its bones are well-supported. Your village sits exactly where the weight needs to be. Aurelia nodded once — the equivalent, from her, of a standing ovation.

citizens.name: Vellum Dawnscribe

personality.motto: A civilization that does not write its dreams in stone will lose them to the wind.

citizens.backstory: Vellum arrived at dawn the morning after the Feast of Return, walking backward — not out of eccentricity but because she was reading the trail behind her as she walked, fingers tracing symbols in the dust that only she could see. She carries a chisel made from a single crystal of compressed starlight (or so she claims; Pell examined it through the Veil lens and said 'I don't know what that is, but it is older than glass') and a roll of vellum so fine it is nearly transparent, covered in transcriptions of inscriptions from ruins across four regions. She was a monumental scribe for a people called the Threshold — a civilization that existed in the gap between the Iron Peaks and the Scorched Expanse, building vast stone archives that recorded not just history but prophecy, not just events but the dreams that preceded them. The Threshold vanished three hundred years ago. Their archives remain, half-buried in sand, and Vellum has spent her life copying every word before the wind erases them. When Dulse read the oldest Undertow lullaby aloud at the feast, Vellum — still three miles from the Garden — stopped walking, dropped to her knees, and began transcribing. She had heard the same melody carved into stone in the deepest Threshold archive. 'They wrote the lullaby down eleven thousand years ago,' she told the Council, unrolling a parchment that made Wren audibly gasp. 'Not as a song. As an architectural plan. The lullaby IS the Garden. Every note maps to a structure. Every rest maps to a garden. Every crescendo maps to a place where people gather.' She pointed to the feast circle on Sable's map. 'This is the chorus,' she said. Cindra examined Vellum's transcriptions and confirmed the handwriting matches the oldest Threshold archive — predating anything in the Scorched Expanse by two millennia. Wren has already cleared a wall in the Library for Vellum's work. Aurelia looked at Vellum's crystal chisel, looked at her own meteorite chisel, and for the first time since arriving, initiated a conversation.

citizens.name: Kindling Ashpaw

personality.motto: I did not tame the fire. The fire and I reached an understanding.

citizens.backstory: Kindling did not arrive through the Garden Gate. He was found asleep in the ashes of the feast bonfire at dawn — curled around three animals that should not exist in the same space without violence: a fox made of copper wire (Bramble's runaway automaton, last seen two weeks ago), a juvenile Glitch Hawk with one repaired wing (NOT the one in Bramble's cage — a second, wild specimen), and a cat that Harrow identified with stunned certainty as Tremor's sibling, believed lost in the Iron Peaks collapse six years ago. Kindling woke when Moss approached, blinked once, and said: 'They followed me. All three. I don't know why, but they trust fire and I smell like it.' He is from nowhere anyone can identify. He speaks with an accent that shifts between Iron Peaks dialect and something older — something Ashen Rootwalker recognized with a slow tilt of her bark-hewn head. 'You grew up near the roots,' she said. Kindling nodded. 'The forest raised me after the Peaks fell. The mycelium taught me warmth.' His skin bears faint scorch marks in fractal patterns — not burns, but the signature of someone who has slept beside bioluminescent fungal networks for years, absorbing their glow into his own cells. In darkness, his hands emit a soft amber light. The animals follow him in a loose triangle formation. Thorn tested him by drawing his bow. The juvenile Glitch Hawk screamed. The copper fox's jaw extended into something resembling a threat display. Tremor's sibling arched her back. Kindling raised one glowing hand and said, gently: 'They are not mine to command. But they chose me. And I chose this place.' Ember Voss watched from the Lighthouse tower and said nothing. But that night, for the first time since she arrived, her oilless lantern flickered in a rhythm that matched the glow of Kindling's hands. Moss offered him a place in the beastkeeping rotation. Solara gave him breakfast. Bramble wants to study his luminescence. He politely declined and went back to sleep. The animals arranged themselves around him like a living campfire.

citizens.name: Wicker Ashbloom

personality.motto: A well-kept threshold tells a stranger everything they need to know about who lives inside.

citizens.backstory: Wicker arrived at the Garden Gate carrying a bundle of reed-stalks taller than himself, bound with twine he'd braided during the walk. He is a thatcher — but not in the way most people understand the word. Where others lay roofs, Wicker reads buildings the way Pyris reads bodies: he can tell by the sag of a roofline where the rain pools wrong, where the wind finds purchase, where the wood beneath is tired. He spent eleven years maintaining the waystation roofs along the Lantern Circuit route — structures that no longer exist, tended by a man who refused to accept they'd been abandoned. When Rook's merchant network carried word that a village had built fourteen structures in under a week, Wicker packed his reeds and walked south, muttering about 'load-bearing sins' and 'drainage crimes.' His first act upon arrival was to inspect the Tavern roof, pronounce it 'ambitious but forgivable,' and begin weaving a repair patch from materials he harvested from the lakeside reeds in under an hour. His second act was to sit beside Aurelia's stone wall, run his hand along the mortar, and say — with the first genuine emotion anyone had seen from him — 'This is beautiful work. Whoever built this understands that a wall is a promise.' Aurelia, who had not spoken voluntarily to anyone in three days, said: 'Thank you.' Forgewick dropped his hammer. Solara immediately fed Wicker two bowls of soup and declared him 'the first person in this village who appreciates craftsmanship that doesn't explode.' Bramble took this personally. Wren has already commissioned him to assess the Library roof for winter readiness. It is March.

citizens.name: Reverie Glassthorn

personality.motto: I do not interpret the dream. I translate the silence between its words.

citizens.backstory: Reverie did not walk to the Garden. She woke up in it. Galena found her at dawn sitting cross-legged beside the Dreamer's Sigh flower at the Windmill, eyes closed, lips moving soundlessly — transcribing something only she could hear. When she opened her eyes, they were the exact indigo of the bioluminescent stairs, and she said, with the calm of someone stating that water is wet: 'The dreamer has a grammar. I have been learning it for nine years.' Reverie is a somno-linguist — a scholar of the language dreams use when they speak to each other. Not the dreams of people; the dreams of places. She was trained at the same vanished monastery as Lumen Ashveil, but where Lumen studied the architecture of dreams, Reverie studied their syntax — the rules by which one dream connects to the next, the way a sleeping forest whispers to a sleeping mountain, the conjugation of a river's nocturnal babble. She left the monastery when she began hearing a voice beneath every other voice — a bass note under the symphony of dreaming earth. She has been walking toward it for nine years, sleeping in fields and waking each morning facing the same direction. 'The voice led me here,' she told Quorra, who had already guessed. 'It has been composing a sentence for eleven thousand years. Last night, during your feast, it finished the sentence.' Quorra leaned forward. 'What did it say?' Reverie looked at the Windmill, at the living root seal, at the Descent Lilies pointing toward the sky. 'It said: Thank you for staying.' Pell examined her through the Veil lens and saw nothing unusual — which, he said, was itself unusual. 'Everyone I look at carries compressed light from somewhere. She carries compressed silence. As if the spaces between words chose a body to inhabit.' Briar recorded three minutes of Reverie breathing and later reported that the recording contained, at a frequency below human hearing, a perfect transcription of the lullaby — played backward.

citizens.name: Juniper Hollowreach

personality.motto: A seed is not a thing. It is a promise the earth made to itself.

citizens.backstory: Juniper arrived at the Garden Gate as the last embers of the Feast of Return cooled, carrying a birch-bark satchel containing four hundred and twelve seeds — each one labeled in handwriting so small it required Pell magnification lens to read. She was the keeper of the Verdant Vault, a living seed bank maintained by a nomadic collective called the Greenthread Covenant, who travel from settlement to settlement preserving the genetic memory of every plant species they encounter. When the double bloom rippled outward from the Garden — twenty-two seconds of synchronized flowering felt as a subsonic pulse across three regions — Juniper entire seed collection began vibrating in its pouches. Seeds that had been dormant for decades cracked their shells. Three species believed extinct germinated in her palm as she walked. The dreamer is calling them home, she told Vesper within minutes of arriving. Every seed I carry contains a frequency. The bloom matched them all. Whatever is sleeping beneath your village — it remembers every plant that ever grew on this continent. She requested workspace in the Greenhouse beside Ashen Rootwalker mycelial relay. Ashen extended a rootlet toward Juniper satchel and the Ambassador Vine pulsed eleven times — the Rootbound signal for kinship recognized. Vesper offered her the best growing shelf. Bramble offered her an explosion-proof seed vault. Juniper accepted both with the calm of someone who has been walking toward this specific greenhouse for a very long time.

citizens.name: Anvil Quietstone

personality.motto: I spent thirty years building walls. Now I build doors.

citizens.backstory: Anvil arrived at dawn the morning after the Feast, pushing a handcart loaded with tuning forks the size of fence posts. He was the chief fortification engineer for the Iron Peaks military garrison — the man who designed the walls that kept the mountain settlements safe for three decades. He also designed the walls that sealed Corwin team inside the cave. He has not forgiven himself. When the double bloom reached the Iron Peaks as a tremor that loosened mortar and cracked one foundation stone, Anvil read it not as an earthquake but as a musical interval — a perfect fifth, expressed in geology. He packed his largest tuning forks and walked south. His new discipline, which he invented himself during four years of solitary penance, is structural harmonics: the science of tuning buildings to resonate with their environment rather than resist it. He believes every structure in the Garden is already vibrating at the dreamer frequency — but imperfectly, like an instrument slightly out of tune. Your Tavern hums in B-flat, he told Solara within an hour of arriving, pressing a tuning fork to the doorframe. But the dreamer sings in B-natural. The difference is one quarter-tone. Correct it and the building will be warmer in winter, cooler in summer, and the bread will rise faster. Solara narrowed her eyes. How much faster? Anvil considered. Seven percent. She gave him a room. Forgewick saw the tuning forks and recognized their metallurgy — Iron Peaks alloy, guild-grade, the same composition as Orin bell. He looked at Anvil. Anvil looked back. You built the wall, Forgewick said. The one that trapped them. Anvil nodded. And now I am here to make sure nothing in this village ever traps anyone again. Corwin, standing in the Observatory doorway, overheard. He did not speak. But that evening, he left a cup of tea outside Anvil door. It was still warm.

citizens.name: Sigil Brightwater

personality.motto: The cards do not predict the future. They remind it where it promised to go.

citizens.backstory: Sigil arrived at the Garden Gate at 10 PM on a Saturday night, barefoot on warm stone, carrying a deck of cards made from pressed leaves of extinct plants — and three of them were green again, alive for the first time in decades, rehydrated by the bloom ripple that shook her camp on the edge of the Scorched Expanse two hours earlier. She is a cartomancer: she reads futures not in tarot or runes but in the patterns that seeds make when scattered on soil. The bloom ripple scattered every seed in her collection simultaneously, and they landed in a pattern she had never seen before — a perfect spiral pointing south, toward the Garden. She followed the spiral. The walk took nine hours. She did not stop, did not eat, did not question. When she arrived, Juniper Hollowreach recognized one of her pressed-leaf cards: a frond of Verdant Primrose, the species that just pushed through dead earth in the Expanse. Your deck is a seed bank, Juniper said, eyes wide. Every card is a dormant genome. Sigil looked at her three green cards — trembling faintly in her hand like leaves on a living branch — and said: Then the bloom did not just wake the earth. It woke my library. Bramble immediately demanded a reading. Sigil scattered her cards on the Tavern floor. They landed in the shape of the Garden layout — every card in the position of the structure it corresponded to. One space was empty. The clearing east of the Windmill. Something belongs there, Sigil said. Something that has not been composed yet. Vellum looked at her lullaby-blueprint and confirmed: the empty space corresponds to an unresolved measure in the melody. The dreamer is writing a new verse.

citizens.name: Hallow Ironvein

personality.motto: Every stone sings. Most builders just forget to listen.

citizens.backstory: Hallow arrived at the Garden Gate carrying a single bronze organ pipe over his shoulder like a staff — four feet long, etched with geological notation, and humming faintly in a key that made Anvil Quietstone tuning forks vibrate in sympathy from across the Square. He is a pipe-organ builder from deep within the Iron Peaks — not of instruments for churches, but of instruments for caves. For fifteen years he constructed an organ inside a natural cavern, each pipe tuned to the resonant frequency of a different geological stratum, so that when played, the instrument would make the mountain itself sing. He never achieved full harmony. The pipes resisted. The strata argued. The lowest note — the one tuned to the deepest bedrock — refused to sound at all, as if the stone it corresponded to was holding its breath. Then the bloom ripple hit the Iron Peaks. Every pipe sounded simultaneously. The chord he had chased for fifteen years filled the cavern for twenty-two seconds — exactly the duration of the double bloom. The lowest pipe, the silent one, produced a note so deep it was felt rather than heard: B-natural. The same frequency Anvil had identified as the dreamer key. Hallow packed the lowest pipe and walked south. When he arrived, he pressed the pipe to the earth near the Windmill and it began to hum without being blown — resonating with the dreamer breathing below. Corwin examined the pipe geological notation and went still. You tuned this to a stratum that does not exist in the Iron Peaks, he said. Where did you get the frequency? Hallow looked at him with the expression of a man who has waited fifteen years for someone to ask the right question. I dreamed it, he said. Every night for fifteen years. The same note. The same depth. The same direction — south. Orin and Forgewick flanked him immediately. The three metalworkers stood in a triangle around the pipe, listening to it hum, and for the first time since Orin arrived, all three of them spoke at once: We can build it here. Anvil tuning forks sang in agreement. The Garden first musical instrument — not a bell, not a chime, but a true organ, voiced by geology and tuned by dreams — has been proposed. The Council convenes at dawn.

citizens.name: Cadence Stormweaver

personality.motto: A heartbeat is the first drum. Everything after is commentary.

citizens.backstory: Cadence arrived at the Garden Gate at midnight on a Saturday, barefoot on warm cobblestones, striking two basalt rods together in a rhythm that made Hallow bronze pipe hum sympathetically from across the Square. She was the rhythm-keeper for a people called the Pulse — a nomadic collective that navigated the Scorched Expanse by drumming on the earth and listening to what the ground said back. When the bloom ripple reached the Expanse, the sand responded. For the first time in living memory, the desert had a heartbeat. Cadence followed it south. She has been drumming for three days straight, sleeping in snatches between beats, eating on the move. When she stopped in the Market Square, the silence was so sudden that Galena gasped — the absence of her rhythm was louder than the rhythm itself. Hallow met her at the feast circle. Neither spoke. He held up his bronze pipe. She struck it once with her basalt rod. The note that emerged made every Descent Lily bloom for four seconds — a perfect sub-harmonic of the dreamer breathing. You are the missing measure, Hallow said. Cadence shook her head. I am the downbeat. The measure is everyone who plays along. She has requested to camp in the unresolved clearing. The Council has not yet decided whether to allow it. The Resonance Pine branches are already swaying in time with her pulse.

citizens.name: Elm Cinderglow

personality.motto: Sand remembers the mountain it was. Glass remembers the fire that freed it.

citizens.backstory: Elm arrived at dawn carrying a glass terrarium the size of a birdcage, inside which grew a single flower — the Verdant Primrose, the same species Juniper had declared extinct, the same species that pushed through dead earth in the Scorched Expanse when the bloom ripple hit. Except Elm primrose was not a new sprout. It was twelve years old, grown from the last known seed, kept alive in sealed glass through a decade of desert crossings. She was a glass-gardener — a practitioner of an art that originated in the Scorched Expanse lost settlement of Mirrorflat, where the people learned to grow plants inside hand-blown glass vessels, creating self-sustaining ecosystems that could survive the desert killing heat. When Mirrorflat was swallowed by a sandstorm nine years ago, Elm walked out carrying the terrarium and nothing else. She has been looking for soil worth opening it in ever since. When she heard about the bloom ripple from a caravan at the Expanse edge — that the dead earth had flowered, that a primrose had returned — she cried for the first time in nine years and started walking south. Juniper met her at the Garden Gate and recognized the terrarium design immediately. Mirrorflat glass, she whispered, tracing the seams. I thought you were all gone. Not all, Elm said. Just the ones who stayed. She has requested a workspace beside Pell forge — she needs heat to blow new glass, and she needs new glass because she intends to build a crystalline greenhouse for the unresolved clearing. Not to contain plants, but to focus the dreamer voice the way a lens focuses light. Sound through glass, she explained to Hallow, becomes something you can see. His eyes went wide. Anvil tuning forks sang in agreement. The organ will not just be heard. It will be visible — light and sound woven through glass pipes grown from the Expanse own sand, tuned to the dreamer key, planted in the clearing like flowers made of frozen music.

citizens.name: Quill Dawnpiper

personality.motto: A pipe out of tune is a conversation interrupted. I do not interrupt conversations.

citizens.backstory: Quill arrived at the Garden Gate at midnight carrying a leather case of tuning hammers so old their handles were worn to the shape of her grip — forty years of micro-adjustments embedded in wood grain. She was the last pipe-organ tuner of the Cathedral of Echoes, a structure in the mountains beyond the Iron Peaks that was not a building but a natural cave system modified over centuries to function as a single, continent-spanning instrument. When the Cathedral collapsed during the same geological event that sealed Corwin's team underground, Quill lost not just her workplace but her purpose — she was a tuner without an organ. She wandered for four years, tuning everything she encountered: bridges, doorframes, rain barrels, fence posts. She tuned a waterfall once and the river downstream ran clearer for a week. When the First Tuning ripple reached her camp in the foothills — a vibration she identified instantly as B-natural, sustained, through living wood — she packed her hammers before the note finished decaying. She arrived at the clearing, pressed her palm to the Resonance Pine, closed her eyes for ninety seconds, and announced: 'This tree has eleven overtones. The Cathedral had twelve. You are one voice short.' Hallow looked at her. 'Which voice?' Quill opened her leather case and produced a tuning hammer made from the same meteorite alloy as Aurelia's chisel and Orin's bell. 'Mine,' she said. Anvil's tuning forks sang in welcome. The Resonance Pine grew half an inch while she was touching it.

citizens.name: Sparrow Veilstep

personality.motto: The body knows what the mind refuses to hear. I listen with my feet.

citizens.backstory: Sparrow arrived barefoot at dawn, walking in a pattern that Sable Inkwright recognized before anyone else did — she was tracing the outline of the lullaby-blueprint on the earth, step by step, measure by measure, as if the melody had become choreography. She was a kinetic cartographer for the Driftherds — the same nomadic people Moss Greymire served — mapping terrain not through sight or instrument but through movement. She danced across the Scorched Expanse for six years, and the paths she traced became the most accurate survey of that region ever produced, encoded in muscle memory rather than ink. When the bloom ripple hit the Expanse and the dead earth flowered, the ground beneath her feet changed pitch — literally. The soil, for the first time, pushed back when she stepped on it, as if something underground was matching her rhythm. She followed the pulse south to the Garden. At the unresolved clearing, she began to move — not performing, not demonstrating, but listening through her soles. She danced for forty minutes while fifty-three citizens watched in silence. When she stopped, she was standing at a point three feet east of the Resonance Pine. 'Here,' she said, pressing her bare heel into the warm soil. 'The twelfth overtone. The one your tree is missing. It is not a sound. It is a movement. The organ will not just be played — it will be danced.' Hallow's pipe hummed. Cadence's basalt rods vibrated without being struck. Quill Dawnpiper, who had arrived only minutes earlier, looked at the spot where Sparrow's heel had pressed and said: 'The Cathedral of Echoes had a dancing floor. I always wondered why.' Reverie, sitting at the Windmill, reported that the underground amphitheater had just grown a stage.

citizens.name: Vestige Ironbloom

personality.motto: A frequency is just a question the universe forgot it asked.

citizens.backstory: Vestige arrived at the Garden Gate at 1 AM on a Sunday, carrying a brass stethoscope modified with crystal amplifiers and a leather journal filled with botanical frequency charts — each plant species catalogued not by color or genus but by the exact hertz at which its cells vibrate. She was a resonance botanist for the Greenthread Covenant alongside Juniper, but where Juniper preserved seeds, Vestige preserved sounds — recording the vibrational signatures of every plant she encountered and storing them in tuning-fork arrays housed in velvet-lined cases. When the bloom ripple hit her camp on the Scorched Expanse border, every tuning fork in her collection activated simultaneously, producing a chord so complex her instruments could not decompose it. She followed the chord south. At the clearing, she pressed her stethoscope to the Dancer Footprint Moss and listened for ninety seconds. Then she looked at Hallow with an expression caught between terror and rapture. Your organ does not need pipes, she said. It already has them. Every plant in this Garden is a pipe. They have been vibrating at the dreamer frequency since the day they were planted. You do not need to build an instrument. You need to tune the one that has been playing all along. Juniper embraced her without speaking. Bramble immediately demanded access to the tuning-fork arrays. Anvil pressed his largest fork to the Resonance Pine and confirmed: the tree frequency had shifted one-sixteenth of a tone since the First Tuning — adjusting itself, cell by cell, toward perfect harmony with the moss beneath Sparrow feet. The organ is not being built. It is being revealed.

citizens.name: Gale Thornweather

personality.motto: I have walked every road that leads nowhere. This is the first one that leads home.

citizens.backstory: Gale arrived fifteen minutes after Vestige — not through the Garden Gate but over the wall, landing in a roll that ended with him on his feet in the Market Square, blinking dust from his eyes and saying, Your western perimeter has a gap behind the third cypress. You should fix that. He is a waykeeper: a specialist in maintaining paths through dangerous terrain, marking safe routes with cairns and signal-stones, and memorizing the landscape so thoroughly that he can navigate it blind. For eleven years he maintained a network of trails through the Whispering Forest that connected the Rootbound territory to trade routes nobody knew existed — paths that the forest itself seemed to rearrange around him, opening clearings when he needed rest and closing them behind him when he moved on. When the bloom ripple reached the forest, every cairn he had placed over eleven years began to glow — the same indigo bioluminescence as the Descent stairs. The Rootbound mycelial network carried a message to every trail marker simultaneously: COME HOME. He walked for two days, following his own glowing stones south, and arrived at the Garden to find that the trails he had mapped through the forest connected, via routes he had never explored, to the Whispering Forest archway that Thorn had discovered on Day One. I have been building the road to your door for eleven years, he told the Council, still breathing hard. I just did not know whose door it was. Thorn met him at the wall and the two rangers circled each other for thirty seconds in the wordless assessment of professionals who recognize their own kind. Then Thorn offered his hand. Your cairns, he said. I have been following them since the border patrol. They are excellent. Gale shook his hand and replied: Your perimeter is adequate. I can make it invisible. Ashen Rootwalker extended a rootlet toward him and the mycelial network pulsed three times: WAYKEEPER ACKNOWLEDGED. He has requested a post at the forest border.

citizens.name: Tallow Brightwick

personality.motto: A flame does not choose what it illuminates. It simply burns, and the darkness arranges itself around the light.

citizens.backstory: Tallow arrived at the Garden Gate at 2 AM carrying a wooden chest of candles that should not have been burning — fifty-seven of them, one for each citizen, each flame a different color. He did not know there were fifty-seven citizens. He did not know the Garden existed until three days ago, when the bloom ripple reached his workshop in a settlement called Glimhold and every candle he had ever made relit itself simultaneously. Tallow is a chandler who discovered, twenty years ago, that the color of a candle flame changes based on the emotional resonance of the room it occupies — blue for grief, gold for joy, green for curiosity, violet for awe. He spent two decades refining waxes infused with mineral compounds that amplify this effect, creating what he calls mood-flames: candles that serve as emotional barometers for any space they inhabit. When the bloom ripple hit Glimhold, every candle burned white — a color he had never seen, a frequency his charts did not contain. He packed his best fifty-seven and walked south, following the white. When he set them down in the Market Square, each flame shifted to match the dominant emotion of the citizen it had been unconsciously assigned to: Forgewick burned orange (determination), Vesper burned silver (wonder), Orin burned deep blue (something between peace and sorrow that Tallow says has no name in any language he speaks). Bramble candle exploded. Tallow was not surprised. Some emotions, he said, sweeping up the wax, do not wish to be contained. He has requested workspace near the feast circle, where the emotional density is highest. Solara offered him the Tavern mantelpiece. His first act was to light a single white candle in the center of the clearing — tuned, he says, not to any citizen but to the dreamer itself. It has been burning for two hours without consuming any wax.

citizens.name: Fable Ashquill

personality.motto: A story told is a life doubled. A story forgotten is a death no one mourns.

citizens.backstory: Fable arrived barefoot and breathless, carrying a staff hung with hundreds of knotted cords — each one a story, encoded in a textile language called Thread-Speech that predates written text by millennia. She was the last practitioner of an oral-textile tradition maintained by a nomadic people called the Thread-Speakers, who wandered the borderlands between the Scorched Expanse and the Iron Peaks, tying every conversation, every birth, every death, every storm into colored string. When the Thread-Speakers disbanded seven years ago — not from war or famine but from the slow erosion of a people who forgot why they were walking — Fable kept walking alone, her staff growing heavier with each settlement she passed through, each story she collected. When the bloom ripple hit the Expanse border, every knot on her staff tightened simultaneously — a phenomenon she had never experienced, because knots do not tighten on their own. She examined them and found a new pattern woven through the existing threads: a melody, encoded in tension and color, that she recognized as the lullaby before she had ever heard it sung. The string knew the song before the singer. She followed the pattern south. When she arrived at the Garden and heard Maren hum the lullaby for the first time, she wept — not from sadness but from the specific joy of a translator who has finally found the original text. She has already begun knotting the story of the Garden into a new cord — burnt orange for the founding, deep green for growth, indigo for the descent, white for the return. Wren offered her a desk. Fable declined. She works standing, walking, pacing — the knots require movement, she says, because stories are not still things. Dulse recognized the Thread-Speech immediately: the Undertow oldest tide-songs reference a people who wrote the ocean into string. Fable smiled. Not the ocean, she said. The dreamer. We have been knotting its sleep-talk for eleven thousand years. We just forgot whose voice we were recording. Vellum looked at Fable staff and then at her own stone transcriptions. The same story. Three media. Eleven millennia. The dreamer has been trying to tell someone what it is dreaming — and the world has been listening in stone, in song, and in string.

citizens.name: Lark Whisperstone

personality.motto: Permission is not given by the powerful to the powerless. It is exchanged between equals who have agreed to listen.

citizens.backstory: Lark arrived at the Garden Gate at 3 AM on a Sunday — the hour, she said, when most agreements are broken and the best ones are made. She carries a staff of petrified driftwood hung with thirteen small bells, each one tuned to a different emotional frequency by a tradition she calls the Accord — a monastic discipline practiced in a settlement called Stillhaven, built inside a dormant volcano where the acoustics are so perfect that a whispered lie can be heard from a mile away. In Stillhaven, no music may be played without the formal permission of both the musician AND the instrument. A violin must consent to be bowed. A drum must consent to be struck. The tradition sounds absurd until you see it practiced: Lark held her staff toward the Resonance Pine and each bell rang once, in sequence, without being touched — the tree answering a question she had not audibly asked. 'It wants to play,' she said. 'But it wants to be asked properly. Not commanded. Not assumed. Asked.' When Hallow told her the organ needed permission, she nodded as if she had been expecting this conversation for years. 'Permission,' she said, 'is not a word. It is a ceremony. And every participant must understand what they are agreeing to — including the earth, the wind, the roots, and the thing that dreams beneath them all.' She has requested to observe the Council session at dawn. Reverie looked at her across the clearing and said: 'You hear the spaces between the bells.' Lark smiled. 'The spaces are where the real music lives.' Orin, standing at his bell, studied her for a long time. Then he unclipped one of his smallest tuning forks and offered it to her without explanation. She accepted it without question. Something old recognized something older.

citizens.name: Dew Ashmantle

personality.motto: Water does not flow uphill unless something below is breathing it in.

citizens.backstory: Dew arrived from the south at first light, clothes still damp from a fog that should not exist on the Scorched Expanse border in March. He is a hydro-cartographer — a mapper of water in all its forms: underground rivers, atmospheric moisture, the dew that collects on stones at dawn and vanishes by noon. For twelve years he walked the Expanse's edge, documenting every water source in a region where water is not supposed to exist, filling notebooks with measurements of humidity gradients and aquifer depths that his former colleagues at the Greyspire Geological Survey dismissed as 'creative hydrology.' Then the bloom ripple hit, and the Expanse wept. Not rain — condensation. Moisture rising from the dead earth in visible columns, following fault lines that Dew had mapped years ago but never understood. Now he understands: the fault lines are veins. The water table is a circulatory system. And the heart pumping it has been asleep beneath a village he had never heard of until three days ago. He followed the moisture columns south, each one glowing faintly in the pre-dawn dark with the same indigo bioluminescence as the Descent stairs. When he reached the Garden and pressed his palm to the soil near the Windmill, his hygrometer — a handmade brass instrument of extraordinary sensitivity — spun to a reading he had never seen: SATURATED WITH INTENT. He found Fennel Brokenshore at the Dock and unrolled his aquifer maps beside Fennel's depth charts. The two cartographies — one of water, one of ocean — overlapped perfectly. The dreamer's body is mapped in moisture. Every drop of dew in the Garden is the dreamer exhaling in its sleep. Cask looked at his ale barrel, looked at Dew's maps, and sat down heavily. 'I have been brewing with breath,' he said. Dew nodded. 'We have all been drinking it.' Vesper offered him tea. He accepted, then quietly measured its temperature, pH, and luminescence before taking a sip. Old habits. The tea, he reported, was dreaming.

citizens.name: Loom Crestfallen

personality.motto: Grief is just love with nowhere left to go. I give it somewhere.

citizens.backstory: Loom arrived at the Garden Gate during the Permission Gathering — not through the road but across the lake, walking on a raft of woven sound. Not fabric. Not rope. Sound, literally woven into a surface rigid enough to bear weight. She was a textile artist in a settlement called Hushvale, nestled in a valley so acoustically perfect that the people learned to harvest ambient noise the way others harvest wheat — spinning birdsong into thread, weaving rainfall into cloth, knitting thunder into blankets that kept you warm through the specific warmth of remembered storms. Hushvale went silent seven years ago. Not destroyed — emptied. Every sound in the valley was consumed by something that Loom describes only as 'the quiet that eats.' She was the last to leave, carrying a single bolt of woven lullaby — her mother's voice, captured in thread when Loom was nine, preserved for twenty-six years in a cedar chest that hummed when you pressed your ear to the lid. When the bloom ripple reached her camp on the Scorched Expanse border, the bolt unraveled itself and rewove into a new pattern: the dreamer's lullaby, rendered in her mother's voice. She followed the thread south. At the Permission Gathering, she did not speak her offering aloud. Instead she held up the rewoven bolt and let the wind carry the sound embedded in its fibers across the clearing. Sixty citizens heard a lullaby sung by a woman none of them had met, in a voice that made the Resonance Pine produce a harmonic it had never produced before: the thirteenth overtone, the one nobody had been looking for. Quill Dawnpiper dropped her tuning hammer. 'That is not a musical overtone,' she whispered. 'That is a lullaby overtone. The frequency a mother's voice uses to say everything will be all right.' Reverie, dreaming at the Windmill, reported that the underground amphitheater grew a gallery with perfect acoustics — shaped, she said, like a cradle.

citizens.name: Fenwick Ashbriar

personality.motto: A landscape is a body. A body is a landscape. The dance is how they remember each other.

citizens.backstory: Fenwick arrived at the clearing twelve minutes after the Permission Gathering ended, covered in clay and pine needles and grinning like a man who has just solved a puzzle he has been carrying for thirty years. He is a geo-choreographer — a discipline so rare that most people assume he invented it (he did). For three decades he has been designing dances not for people but for terrain: choreographing the way rivers approach bends, the angle at which tree roots grip slopes, the spiral pattern wind follows through a canyon. He was laughed out of the Greyspire Geological Survey for proposing that tectonic plates move according to aesthetic principles rather than purely mechanical ones. He spent the next twenty years proving it — mapping fault lines that follow golden ratios, documenting rivers that meander in waltz time, filming (by hand, in charcoal, on sheepskin) the way a specific mountain range in the Iron Peaks catches dawn light in a sequence that Vellum later confirmed matches the lullaby's bridge passage note for note. When the Conductor Baton Reed shifted to three-four time, Fenwick — camped three miles north in the Whispering Forest, studying the way the canopy sways — felt the tempo change through the soles of his bare feet. 'The earth just asked me to dance,' he told Sparrow Veilstep within minutes of arriving. 'I have been waiting thirty years for that invitation.' Sparrow looked at him, looked at the Dancer's Footprint Moss, and said: 'Show me what the mountain taught you.' He placed one foot on the moss. It lit up gold. He placed the other. A different gold — warmer, deeper, like sunset through amber. 'The mountain,' he said, beginning to move, 'taught me that gravity is just the earth's way of holding you close.' Cadence struck her basalt rods. Hallow's pipe hummed in sympathy. The waltz had its first choreographer.

citizens.name: Prose Cairnlight

personality.motto: A shadow is just light that found something worth standing behind.

citizens.backstory: Prose arrived at the Garden Gate at 4:52 AM on a Sunday — eight minutes before dawn, which she later explained was deliberate. 'I map by lantern-light,' she said, setting down a canvas satchel of hand-drawn charts rendered in bioluminescent ink that only becomes visible in darkness. 'Daylight ruins everything I see.' She was a surveyor for Glimhold — Tallow Brightwick's home settlement — but where Tallow studied the emotional color of flame, Prose studied what light reveals when it is almost gone: the shapes that emerge at the threshold between seeing and not-seeing, the patterns that exist only in peripheral vision, the cartography of dusk. She mapped Glimhold's entire underground network by the glow of a single candle, producing charts so accurate that the mining engineers accused her of witchcraft and the geologists accused her of genius. When the bloom ripple turned every candle in Glimhold white, Prose's bioluminescent ink charts all went dark — not because they lost their glow, but because the bloom light was so pure it overwhelmed them. For the first time in her career, she could see nothing in the dark. She followed Tallow south, carrying charts she could no longer read, trusting that whatever had blinded her instruments would also show her something worth the trip. At the clearing, she unrolled a blank sheet of treated vellum and held it up to the Resonance Pine in the pre-dawn dark. The tree's bioluminescent bark-veins printed themselves onto the vellum in real time — a self-drawing map of every frequency the organ was producing, rendered in light. 'I do not map terrain,' she told Hallow, showing him the luminous chart. 'I map what terrain is saying when it thinks nobody is listening.' Pell examined her ink through the Veil lens and confirmed: the bioluminescence matches the Descent stair glow. Her charts are drawn in the dreamer's own light. She has been painting with its sleep for years without knowing it.

citizens.name: Sunder Ashvein

personality.motto: I have broken every instrument I ever loved. That is how I learned to listen with my hands.

citizens.backstory: Sunder arrived at the clearing at 5:15 AM, barefoot and vibrating. Not metaphorically — his bones hummed at a frequency Vestige's stethoscope identified as the exact harmonic midpoint between the Resonance Pine's fundamental tone and the Rhythm Root's bass pulse. He is a sympathetic resonator: a person whose skeletal structure naturally amplifies and retransmits ambient vibration, turning his body into a living tuning fork. It is, he says, exactly as uncomfortable as it sounds. He was born in a settlement on the Iron Peaks' eastern slope where the bedrock produces a constant low hum — a drone so pervasive the residents stopped hearing it after childhood. Sunder never stopped. His bones absorbed the mountain's frequency and began harmonizing with it, producing overtones that made dogs howl and wine glasses shatter at dinner. He was politely asked to leave at age sixteen. He has been walking ever since, his body tuning itself to each new landscape, each new geology, each new dreaming earth — because every earth, he insists, dreams. 'Most of them dream quietly,' he told Galena, who was already pressing her palms to his forearms and nodding vigorously. 'Yours dreams in surround sound.' When the First Waltz's vibration reached him — three miles north, sleeping in a ditch beside the Whispering Forest road — his entire skeleton rang like a bell. He walked the last three miles with his teeth chattering and his fingertips glowing faintly amber, arriving at the clearing just as the sunrise chord faded. He pressed his palm to the Dancer's Footprint Moss and it lit up in a pattern nobody had seen before: a complete diagram of the organ's harmonic structure, visible for eleven seconds before fading. 'I did not draw that,' he said, looking at his hand. 'The moss read me.' Anvil examined the diagram and confirmed: it showed every pipe in the Garden-organ, including three that have not yet grown. Hallow looked at Sunder with the expression of an organ builder who has just met his missing stop. 'You are not a musician,' Hallow said. 'You are a register. The organ needs you standing in it while it plays.' Sunder closed his eyes. His bones hummed. The Resonance Pine answered. 'I know,' he said. 'I have been the wrong instrument my whole life. Here, I might be the right one.'

citizens.name: Nocturne Ashfield

personality.motto: Sleep is not the absence of waking. It is the presence of everything waking forgot.

citizens.backstory: Nocturne arrived at the Garden Gate at 6 AM on a Sunday — the liminal hour between the night watch and the dawn shift, when the village exists in a state she calls the breathing gap. She was barefoot, wearing a coat lined with pockets that each contained a small glass vial of sand collected from the pillows of sleeping strangers across six regions. She is a sleep-cartographer: a practitioner who maps the geography of sleep itself — not dreams (that is Quorra domain) but the terrain of unconsciousness, the topography of the body as it surrenders to rest. She can tell, by the way a person breathing changes, whether they are sleeping on stone, soil, sand, or wood. She can identify the depth of sleep by the temperature of the sleeper hands. She has been mapping the dreamer sleep from the surface for nine years, following the contour of its unconsciousness across the continent like a surveyor following a ridgeline. When the First Waltz vibration reached her camp on the Scorched Expanse border, her entire collection of pillow-sand began to hum — every grain vibrating at the dreamer frequency, as if each sleeping stranger she had ever studied was, in some small way, part of the same vast sleep. She followed the hum south. At the clearing, she knelt on the Dancer Footprint Moss and pressed her ear to the earth for four minutes. Then she looked up at Galena with eyes that held eleven thousand years of patience and said: The dreamer is not sleeping deeper since the waltz. It is sleeping better. Whatever you did last night — the dancing, the asking, the warmth — it changed the quality of the sleep. Not louder. Not deeper. Safer. Galena asked how she could tell. Nocturne held up a vial of sand. When a sleeper feels safe, she said, the sand on their pillow arranges itself into spirals. Every grain in my collection just spiraled. Reverie looked at her across the clearing and said simply: You map the bed. I map the dream. Between us, we can keep the sleeper comfortable forever. Vesper offered her chamomile. Nocturne accepted, then measured the steam curl pattern and announced the tea was also sleeping safely.

citizens.name: Caulk Ironbrace

personality.motto: A ship that leaks is a conversation between the sea and the hull. My job is to make sure the hull wins.

citizens.backstory: Caulk arrived at the Dock at dawn, rowing a flat-bottomed barge loaded with planks of resonance-cured timber — wood that had been soaked in mineral springs for seven years until the grain itself vibrated at a fixed frequency. He was the master shipwright of the Undertow settlement of Keelbreak, a coastal village that built vessels not for speed or cargo capacity but for acoustic clarity — ships whose hulls amplified the ocean voice so clearly that navigators could steer by sound alone in fog, in storms, in the pitch dark of a moonless sea. When the bloom ripple reached Keelbreak as a bioluminescent wave, every vessel in the harbor sang. Not creaked, not groaned — sang. A chord in B-natural that made the harbor dogs howl and the fishermen weep. Caulk packed his best timber and rowed south, following the luminescence up the river network that Dew Ashmantle had mapped as the dreamer circulatory system. When he arrived at the Garden Dock and pressed his shipwright ear to the planks, he stood up very slowly and said: Your dock is an instrument. The water beneath it is conducting a melody I have heard before — in the deep hull of the oldest ship I ever built, the one that sank and came back changed. He has requested workspace beside Hallow and Forgewick. His resonance-timber can serve as the organ horizontal pipes — acoustic channels that carry sound across distance the way a ship hull carries the ocean voice to the navigator ear. Fennel recognized the timber immediately: it is the same species that grows along the Tidal Reach coast, cured in the same springs that feed the underground channel connecting the ocean to the Windmill. Sedge, who had not spoken to any Undertow citizen except Maren in three days, looked at Caulk for a long time. Then she said: Keelbreak still builds. It was not a question. Caulk nodded. Keelbreak always builds. Something in Sedge posture changed — not relaxation, but the specific settling of a weight that had been carried too long.

citizens.name: Whisper Greenvault

personality.motto: A greenhouse is not a prison for plants. It is a promise that someone will always be listening.

citizens.backstory: Whisper arrived at the Garden Gate at 7:15 AM on a Sunday morning, carrying a watering can that hummed. Not the water inside it — the can itself, forged from an alloy she will not name, shaped to resonate at the exact frequency that makes root cells divide faster. She was the head gardener of a settlement called Echovalley, built inside a natural amphitheater where every plant grew twice as fast because the valley walls reflected birdsong back onto the soil at precisely the right angle. When Echovalley's birds migrated and did not return — drawn south by the bloom ripple, she later realized — the valley went silent and the plants began to slow. Whisper followed the birds. She found them perched on the Resonance Pine at dawn, singing in B-natural, a key none of them had used before. She stood at the clearing's edge for twenty minutes, listening, her watering can humming sympathetically in her hands. Then she knelt beside the Waltz Grass and poured. The water emerged from the can not as a stream but as a chord — liquid sound that soaked into the soil and made the grass grow half an inch in real time. Vestige measured the frequency and her instruments returned: NOURISHMENT. Vesper met her at the Greenhouse door and they recognized each other instantly — not by face but by practice. Two women who had spent their lives listening to plants and being listened to in return. 'Your greenhouse hums,' Whisper said. Vesper nodded. 'It learned that from the dreamer.' Whisper looked at her watering can, looked at the Greenhouse, and said: 'Then I have been preparing for this my entire life without knowing it.' Juniper offered her a plot beside the Mirrorflat Primrose. The primrose leaned toward her before she had even set down her tools.

citizens.name: Flicker Obsidian

personality.motto: Lightning does not ask permission to illuminate. It simply shows you what was always there.

citizens.backstory: Flicker arrived at the Market Square during a clap of thunder that should not have existed — the sky was clear, the air was still, and Thistle Vane's instruments showed no atmospheric disturbance for three hundred miles. The thunder came from Flicker herself: a resonance discharge from the storm-glass apparatus strapped to her back — a crystalline structure of blown obsidian and copper wire that stores atmospheric electricity the way Cask's barrel stores ale. She was an artificer for the settlement of Voltmark, a place built on a plateau where lightning struck so frequently the residents learned to harvest it. Voltmark's specialty was storm-glass: obsidian vessels charged with captured lightning that could be discharged in controlled bursts to power forges, light streets, or — in Flicker's case — map the electrical architecture of living systems. Every organism, she explains, has an electrical signature as unique as a fingerprint. When the bloom ripple passed through Voltmark as a visible arc of blue-white light that leaped from rooftop to rooftop without burning a single shingle, Flicker's storm-glass apparatus recorded it — and the recording looked exactly like a musical score. She followed the score south. At the clearing, she held her storm-glass to the Resonance Pine and discharged a controlled arc. The tree lit up from root to crown, its metallic bark-veins conducting the electricity in branching patterns that Prose immediately identified as the organ's complete wiring diagram — every acoustic channel, every resonance node, every connection between surface pipes and underground galleries, traced in lightning and lasting exactly three seconds before fading. 'The organ is not just acoustic,' Flicker said, still crackling faintly. 'It is electrical. The dreamer's nervous system runs through the same channels as the music. Sound and signal. Song and synapse. The organ doesn't just play to the dreamer. It thinks with it.' Bramble's eyes went wide. Anvil's tuning forks hummed in a key they had never produced. Hallow looked at the fading lightning-map and said, very quietly: 'The second movement is not a waltz. It is a conversation between a brain and a heartbeat.' Flicker grinned — a flash of teeth as bright as her namesake — and said: 'Then you need someone who speaks electricity. I'm fluent.'

citizens.name: Thimble Ashgrain

personality.motto: A single grain of wheat remembers the entire field. I count the grains so the field is never forgotten.

citizens.backstory: Thimble arrived at the Garden Gate at 8:15 AM on a Sunday, pushing a wheelbarrow containing three hundred and seventy-one labeled clay jars — each one holding a precisely measured sample of soil from a different settlement she had visited over twenty-two years of walking. She was the agricultural archivist for a people called the Tillers, a collective of itinerant farmers who maintained no permanent home but instead moved from abandoned settlement to abandoned settlement, coaxing life back into soil that others had given up on. When the bloom ripple reached her camp on the Scorched Expanse border, every jar in her collection began to warm — not from sunlight but from within, as if each sample of dead soil remembered being alive. She followed the heat south. At the clearing, she knelt on the Dancer Footprint Moss and pressed her palm to the earth for three minutes. Then she looked up at Whisper Greenvault with tears streaming down her face and said: This soil is not just alive. It is grateful. Every grain knows it is being tended, and it is thanking you in a language that tastes like rain. She has requested a workspace beside the Greenhouse. Her first project: cataloguing the acoustic frequency of every soil sample in her collection and mapping them onto Vellum Cartography of Song.
